title: Western Music Theory Roadmap
shortDescription: A comprehensive roadmap to learn Western music theory from beginner to advanced levels.
longDescription: This roadmap guides learners through Western music theory from the absolute basics (the musical alphabet) all the way to contemporary topics like jazz harmony and pitch-class set theory. Sections build on each other, but ear training runs in parallel with everything else and should be practiced from day one. Each topic includes objectives, common mistakes, practice tips, free online resources, and a short assessment.

sections.title: Fundamentals of Notation
sections.shortDescription: Start your journey with pitch, the staff, note durations, and accidentals.
sections.longDescription: This section introduces the foundational tools used to read and write Western music: the musical alphabet, the staff and clefs, note and rest durations, accidentals, and ledger lines. Master this before moving on — everything else assumes you can read basic notation fluently.

topics.title: Pitch, Octaves, and the Musical Alphabet
topics.shortDescription: Understand pitch, the seven-letter musical alphabet (A–G), and how octaves repeat across registers.
topics.longDescription: This topic introduces the most basic building blocks of Western music: individual pitches. You'll learn the musical alphabet (A through G), why pitches repeat every octave, and how octave numbering works (e.g., scientific pitch notation where middle C is C4).
objectives: Name the seven natural pitches of the musical alphabet
Explain what an octave is and why pitches repeat
Identify the same pitch class across different octaves
commonMistakes: Confusing octave numbering systems (scientific pitch notation vs. Helmholtz vs. instrument-specific conventions)
Thinking the alphabet continues past G instead of cycling back to A
practiceTips: Sing or hum pitches while naming them out loud
If you have access to a keyboard, locate the same letter name in multiple octaves

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 10: The Staff, Clefs, and Ledger Lines

assessments.title: Pitch and Octaves Quiz
assessments.description: Check your understanding of pitch names and octaves.

questions.questionText: How many natural (non-accidental) pitch letter names are there in Western music?
questions.explanation: The musical alphabet uses seven letters: A, B, C, D, E, F, and G. After G it cycles back to A in the next octave.

questions.questionText: Two pitches an octave apart share which property?
questions.explanation: Pitches one octave apart share the same letter name. Acoustically, one is double the frequency of the other.

topics.title: The Staff and Clefs
topics.shortDescription: Read pitches on the five-line staff using treble, bass, and (briefly) C clefs.
topics.longDescription: Learn how the five-line staff represents pitch and how clefs (treble, bass, alto, tenor) anchor specific lines to specific pitches. Master reading notes on the lines and spaces of treble and bass clef as the foundation for reading any sheet music.
objectives: Identify all line and space notes on the treble clef
Identify all line and space notes on the bass clef
Recognize the alto and tenor C clefs and explain their purpose
commonMistakes: Mixing up the order of line/space notes between treble and bass clef
Forgetting that the C clef points to middle C wherever it sits
practiceTips: Use mnemonics (Every Good Boy Does Fine, FACE, etc.) but practice until you no longer need them
Quiz yourself daily on random notes — speed matters as much as accuracy

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 10: The Staff, Clefs, and Ledger Lines

assessments.title: Staff and Clefs Quiz
assessments.description: Test your knowledge of the staff and basic clefs.

questions.questionText: How many lines does a standard musical staff have?
questions.explanation: The standard staff has 5 lines and 4 spaces.

questions.questionText: Which note sits on the bottom line of the treble clef?
questions.explanation: The bottom line of the treble clef is E. The mnemonic 'Every Good Boy Does Fine' (E-G-B-D-F) names the five lines from bottom to top.

topics.title: Note Durations and Rests
topics.shortDescription: Learn the relative values of whole, half, quarter, eighth, and sixteenth notes and their corresponding rests.
topics.longDescription: Note duration is how rhythm is encoded on the page. You'll learn the symbols for whole, half, quarter, eighth, and sixteenth notes (and beyond), their proportional relationships, and the matching rest symbols that indicate silence.
objectives: Recognize note and rest symbols from whole to sixteenth values
Explain the proportional relationships (a half note = two quarter notes, etc.)
Count basic rhythms aloud using these values
commonMistakes: Confusing half rests and whole rests visually (the whole rest hangs below the line, the half rest sits on top)
Forgetting that note duration is relative, not absolute (it depends on tempo)
practiceTips: Clap rhythms before playing them on an instrument
Practice counting subdivisions out loud ("1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and")

assessments.title: Note Durations Quiz
assessments.description: Check your understanding of note and rest values.

questions.questionText: How many eighth notes equal one half note?
questions.explanation: A half note equals two quarter notes, and each quarter note equals two eighth notes — so a half note equals 4 eighth notes.

questions.questionText: Which symbol represents silence in music?
questions.explanation: Rests are the symbols that indicate silence for a specified duration.

topics.title: Accidentals: Sharps, Flats, and Naturals
topics.shortDescription: Use sharps (♯), flats (♭), naturals (♮), and double accidentals to alter pitches.
topics.longDescription: Accidentals raise or lower a pitch by a half step (or a whole step for doubles). You'll learn the symbols, how they apply within a measure, and the distinction between enharmonic equivalents (e.g., F♯ and G♭).
objectives: Apply sharps and flats to raise or lower a pitch by a half step
Understand how an accidental persists for the rest of a measure
Recognize enharmonic equivalents (e.g., C♯ = D♭)
commonMistakes: Forgetting that an accidental applies to all later notes on the same line/space in the measure
Treating enharmonic equivalents as 'the same' in all theoretical contexts — they're often functionally different
practiceTips: Write out chromatic scales using sharps ascending and flats descending to practice both
Always check the key signature before deciding if a note needs an accidental

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 13: Half Steps and Whole Steps

assessments.title: Accidentals Quiz
assessments.description: Test your knowledge of sharps, flats, and naturals.

questions.questionText: A sharp (♯) does what to a pitch?

options.optionText: Lowers it by a half step
Raises it by a half step
Raises it by a whole step

questions.explanation: A sharp raises the pitch by one half step.

questions.questionText: Which pitches are enharmonic equivalents?
questions.explanation: F♯ and G♭ are the same pitch spelled two different ways — that's the definition of enharmonic equivalence.

topics.title: Ledger Lines and Extended Range
topics.shortDescription: Read pitches that extend above or below the staff using ledger lines.
topics.longDescription: Most music goes beyond the five lines of the staff. Ledger lines extend the staff upward and downward, letting you read pitches in extended ranges without changing clefs constantly.
objectives: Read ledger line notes above and below the treble clef
Read ledger line notes above and below the bass clef
Identify middle C in multiple clef contexts
commonMistakes: Losing track of which ledger line represents which pitch when there are many of them
Not noticing that ledger lines are spaced the same distance apart as the staff lines
practiceTips: Memorize landmark notes (middle C, the C two octaves above, etc.) and count from there
Practice reading ledger-line passages on real sheet music, not just isolated notes

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 10: The Staff, Clefs, and Ledger Lines

questions.questionText: Middle C appears on which ledger line relative to the treble clef?

options.optionText: One ledger line above the staff
One ledger line below the staff
Two ledger lines below the staff
On the bottom line of the staff

questions.explanation: Middle C sits on the first ledger line below the treble clef staff.

sections.title: Rhythm and Meter
sections.shortDescription: Master the time dimension of music: beats, time signatures, syncopation, and tempo.
sections.longDescription: This section covers how music is organized in time. You'll learn to read and feel simple and compound time signatures, work with dotted notes and tuplets, recognize syncopation, and interpret tempo and dynamic markings.

topics.title: Beats, Measures, and Bar Lines
topics.shortDescription: Understand the pulse of music: beats, measures, and how bar lines organize them.
topics.longDescription: Music is organized in time through beats (the underlying pulse) and measures (groups of beats separated by bar lines). This topic establishes the temporal framework that time signatures and rhythm depend on.
objectives: Define beat and measure
Recognize bar lines, double bar lines, and final bar lines
Tap a steady pulse while reading along with music
commonMistakes: Conflating beat with note duration (a beat is a time unit, not a specific note value)
practiceTips: Use a metronome from day one — internalizing steady pulse is non-negotiable

assessments.title: Beats and Measures Quiz
assessments.description: Quick check on basic rhythmic organization.

questions.questionText: What does a bar line separate?
questions.explanation: Bar lines divide music into measures (also called bars).

topics.title: Simple Time Signatures
topics.shortDescription: Read and interpret simple time signatures like 2/4, 3/4, and 4/4.
topics.longDescription: Simple time signatures group beats where each beat divides naturally into two parts. You'll learn how the top and bottom numbers work and how to count common simple meters.
objectives: Interpret the top and bottom numbers of a time signature
Count in 2/4, 3/4, and 4/4 confidently
Recognize 'cut time' (alla breve) and 'common time' symbols
commonMistakes: Thinking the bottom number is a count — it's a note value (4 = quarter note gets the beat)
Assuming 4/4 always means the same tempo — time signature ≠ tempo
practiceTips: Conduct as you count — physically moving with the beat reinforces the pattern
Tap the beat with one hand while clapping rhythms with the other

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 14: Simple Time Signatures

assessments.title: Simple Time Signatures Quiz
assessments.description: Check your understanding of simple meters.

questions.questionText: In 3/4 time, how many beats are in each measure and what note value gets the beat?

options.optionText: 3 beats, eighth note gets the beat
3 beats, quarter note gets the beat
4 beats, quarter note gets the beat
3 beats, half note gets the beat

questions.explanation: The top number (3) is the beats per measure; the bottom number (4) means a quarter note gets the beat.

topics.title: Compound Time Signatures
topics.shortDescription: Work with compound time signatures like 6/8, 9/8, and 12/8 where beats divide into three.
topics.longDescription: Compound meters group beats into three subdivisions each, giving them a characteristic 'lilting' feel. You'll learn how to count compound meters and distinguish them from simple meters that just happen to have triplets.
objectives: Identify 6/8, 9/8, and 12/8 as compound duple, triple, and quadruple meters
Count compound meters with the correct pulse grouping
Distinguish between 3/4 and 6/8 by feel and beat grouping
commonMistakes: Counting 6/8 as 'six beats' when it's typically felt as two beats of three eighth notes each
practiceTips: Listen to clear examples (e.g., a jig in 6/8 vs. a waltz in 3/4) to internalize the feel
When counting 6/8 fast, say '1-2-3-4-5-6' but accent 1 and 4

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 15: Compound Time Signatures

assessments.title: Compound Time Signatures Quiz
assessments.description: Test your understanding of compound meter.

questions.questionText: 6/8 time is typically felt as how many main pulses per measure?
questions.explanation: 6/8 is compound duple: two main pulses per measure, each subdividing into three eighth notes.

topics.title: Dotted Notes, Ties, and Tuplets
topics.shortDescription: Extend note durations with dots and ties, and divide them unevenly with tuplets.
topics.longDescription: Dots add half the original duration to a note. Ties connect notes across bar lines or beats to create longer durations. Tuplets (especially triplets) divide a beat into a non-standard number of equal parts.
objectives: Calculate dotted note durations correctly
Use ties to extend notes across barlines and beats
Read and count triplets and other tuplets
commonMistakes: Adding a dot's value to itself instead of to the original note (dot = half of the note's value)
Treating ties and slurs as the same — they look similar but slurs are an articulation marking, not a duration marking
practiceTips: Practice subdividing while playing triplets — count '1-trip-let, 2-trip-let' out loud

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 16: Dotted Notes, Ties, and Borrowed Divisions

assessments.title: Dotted Notes and Ties Quiz
assessments.description: Test your understanding of extended rhythmic values.

questions.questionText: A dotted quarter note equals how many eighth notes?
questions.explanation: A quarter note = 2 eighth notes. The dot adds half its value (one more eighth note). Total = 3 eighth notes.

topics.title: Syncopation
topics.shortDescription: Recognize and perform rhythms that accent weak beats or off-beats.
topics.longDescription: Syncopation creates rhythmic interest by emphasizing notes that fall on weak beats or between beats. It's central to jazz, funk, Latin music, and most popular styles.
objectives: Identify syncopated rhythms in notation
Perform syncopated rhythms while keeping the underlying pulse steady
Recognize syncopation by ear in recorded music
commonMistakes: Losing the underlying pulse when the surface rhythm is highly syncopated
practiceTips: Keep the foot tapping or metronome going — the syncopation only works against a steady reference pulse

questions.questionText: Syncopation generally involves emphasizing what?
questions.explanation: Syncopation places accents or longer notes on beats or parts of beats that are typically weak, creating a 'pulled' or 'against the grain' feel.

topics.title: Tempo Markings and Dynamics
topics.shortDescription: Read tempo terms (Allegro, Andante, etc.) and dynamic markings (p, f, crescendo).
topics.longDescription: Tempo markings tell performers how fast to play; dynamics tell them how loud. Both use Italian terms by tradition. You'll learn the most common markings and what they mean.
objectives: Translate common Italian tempo terms to approximate BPM ranges
Read dynamic markings from pp to ff
Interpret crescendo, decrescendo, and other gradual changes
commonMistakes: Treating tempo terms as exact BPM values — they're ranges and depend on style/era
practiceTips: When practicing, write the BPM at the top of the piece so you commit to a tempo

questions.questionText: Which marking is louder?
questions.explanation: From softest to loudest: pp (pianissimo), p (piano), mf (mezzo-forte), ff (fortissimo).

sections.title: Scales and Key Signatures
sections.shortDescription: Major and minor scales, key signatures, the circle of fifths, and key relationships.
sections.longDescription: Scales are the raw material of melody and harmony. This section covers major scales, the three forms of minor, how key signatures work, the circle of fifths, and the relationships between keys.

topics.title: Half Steps and Whole Steps
topics.shortDescription: The two smallest building blocks of Western scales.
topics.longDescription: Every scale in Western tonal music is built from half steps (semitones) and whole steps (whole tones). Understanding these intervals on the keyboard and the staff is the foundation for all scale construction.
objectives: Identify a half step and a whole step on the keyboard
Identify a half step and a whole step on the staff
Understand that 12 half steps make up an octave
commonMistakes: Forgetting that B-to-C and E-to-F are natural half steps (no black key between them on the keyboard)
practiceTips: Visualize the keyboard layout even if you don't play piano — it makes intervals concrete

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 13: Half Steps and Whole Steps

assessments.title: Half and Whole Steps Quiz
assessments.description: Quick check on the smallest scale intervals.

questions.questionText: Which pair is a natural half step (no accidental needed)?
questions.explanation: E and F are a natural half step apart, as are B and C. The other pairs require an accidental to be a half step.

topics.title: The Major Scale
topics.shortDescription: Build major scales using the W-W-H-W-W-W-H pattern.
topics.longDescription: The major scale is the most fundamental scale in Western music. You'll learn its whole-step/half-step pattern and how to construct it starting from any pitch.
objectives: Construct a major scale from any starting note using the W-W-H-W-W-W-H pattern
Identify major scales by ear (compare to minor)
Name the scale degrees (tonic, supertonic, mediant, etc.)
commonMistakes: Forgetting the half steps between scale degrees 3-4 and 7-8
Using the wrong enharmonic spelling (e.g., writing G♯ instead of A♭ in the key of A♭ major)
practiceTips: Sing scales using solfège (do-re-mi-fa-sol-la-ti-do) to internalize the pattern
Write out all 12 major scales by hand at least once

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 20: The Major Scale

assessments.title: Major Scale Quiz
assessments.description: Check your understanding of major scale construction.

questions.questionText: The major scale pattern of whole (W) and half (H) steps is:
questions.explanation: The major scale pattern is W-W-H-W-W-W-H (whole, whole, half, whole, whole, whole, half).

topics.title: Major Key Signatures
topics.shortDescription: Recognize and write key signatures for all 15 major keys.
topics.longDescription: Key signatures place sharps or flats at the start of every staff to indicate which notes are altered throughout the piece. You'll learn the order of sharps and flats and how to identify the major key from a signature.
objectives: Write the key signature for any major key
Identify the major key from its signature
Recite the order of sharps (F-C-G-D-A-E-B) and flats (B-E-A-D-G-C-F)
commonMistakes: Mixing up the order of sharps and flats (they're reverses of each other)
Confusing enharmonic keys like F♯ major and G♭ major
practiceTips: For sharp keys, the last sharp is the leading tone (one half step below the tonic)
For flat keys, the second-to-last flat is the tonic

assessments.title: Major Key Signatures Quiz
assessments.description: Test your knowledge of major key signatures.

questions.questionText: How many sharps are in the key signature of D major?
questions.explanation: D major has two sharps: F♯ and C♯.

questions.questionText: What is the order of sharps in a key signature?
questions.explanation: Sharps are added in the order F♯, C♯, G♯, D♯, A♯, E♯, B♯.

topics.title: The Circle of Fifths
topics.shortDescription: Use the circle of fifths to navigate keys, key signatures, and relationships.
topics.longDescription: The circle of fifths is a visual map of all 12 pitches arranged by perfect fifths. It's the single most useful tool for understanding key signatures, key relationships, and harmonic motion.
objectives: Draw the circle of fifths from memory
Use it to predict the key signature of any major or minor key
Identify closely related keys at a glance
commonMistakes: Treating the circle as just a memorization tool rather than a relational map
practiceTips: Draw it out daily for a week — by day 7 you won't need to look at a reference

assessments.title: Circle of Fifths Quiz
assessments.description: Test your understanding of the circle.

questions.questionText: Moving clockwise on the circle of fifths from C, the next key is:
questions.explanation: Clockwise motion on the circle adds a sharp each step. C → G (1 sharp) → D (2 sharps), etc.

topics.title: The Natural Minor Scale
topics.shortDescription: Build natural minor scales and understand the W-H-W-W-H-W-W pattern.
topics.longDescription: The natural minor scale shares all its notes with its relative major but starts on a different tonic (the 6th degree of the relative major). You'll learn its construction and characteristic darker sound.
objectives: Construct a natural minor scale from any starting note
Identify the relationship between a minor scale and its relative major
Distinguish major from minor by ear
commonMistakes: Building the minor scale by altering the major rather than recognizing it as its own pattern
practiceTips: Sing minor scales using la-based solfège (la-ti-do-re-mi-fa-sol-la) to feel the minor character

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 21: The Minor Scales

assessments.title: Natural Minor Quiz
assessments.description: Test your understanding of natural minor.

questions.questionText: The natural minor scale pattern is:
questions.explanation: Natural minor: W-H-W-W-H-W-W.

topics.title: Harmonic and Melodic Minor Scales
topics.shortDescription: Two variants of minor that adjust the 6th and 7th scale degrees.
topics.longDescription: Harmonic minor raises the 7th scale degree to create a stronger pull to the tonic (and a characteristic augmented second). Melodic minor raises the 6th and 7th ascending, and reverts to natural minor descending (in the traditional classical form).
objectives: Build harmonic minor scales from any starting note
Build melodic minor scales ascending and descending
Explain why these variants exist (the leading tone problem)
commonMistakes: Forgetting that melodic minor ascends and descends differently in classical practice (jazz uses 'real melodic minor' which raises 6 and 7 both ways)
practiceTips: Sing all three minor scales back to back to internalize the differences

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 21: The Minor Scales

assessments.title: Harmonic and Melodic Minor Quiz
assessments.description: Check your knowledge of minor scale variants.

questions.questionText: Harmonic minor differs from natural minor by:
questions.explanation: Harmonic minor raises the 7th scale degree, creating a leading tone.

topics.title: Relative and Parallel Keys
topics.shortDescription: Understand how keys relate as relatives (shared signature) or parallels (shared tonic).
topics.longDescription: Two keys are relative if they share a key signature (e.g., C major and A minor). They're parallel if they share a tonic but differ in mode (e.g., C major and C minor). These relationships are foundational for composition and analysis.
objectives: Find the relative minor of any major key
Find the parallel minor of any major key
Explain why composers move between these related keys
commonMistakes: Confusing relative and parallel
practiceTips: Memorize the shortcut: relative minor = 6th degree of major (or down a minor third from the tonic)

questions.questionText: What is the relative minor of G major?
questions.explanation: G major and E minor share a key signature (one sharp). E is the sixth scale degree of G major.

sections.title: Intervals
sections.shortDescription: Measure distances between pitches by number and quality — the basis of chords and ear training.
sections.longDescription: Intervals are the building blocks of melody and harmony. This section covers all interval qualities (perfect, major, minor, augmented, diminished), compound intervals, inversion, and aural recognition.

topics.title: Introduction to Intervals
topics.shortDescription: An interval is the distance between two pitches, measured by number and quality.
topics.longDescription: Intervals have two parts: a number (counting the letter names involved) and a quality (perfect, major, minor, augmented, diminished). This topic introduces the concept and how to count generic intervals.
objectives: Count the number of an interval correctly (inclusive)
Distinguish melodic and harmonic intervals
Recognize the unison, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, and octave by counting
commonMistakes: Counting from zero instead of one (a 3rd spans three letter names, including both endpoints)
practiceTips: Use your fingers to count letter names — endpoint to endpoint

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 30: Introduction to Intervals

questions.questionText: C to G is a what?
questions.explanation: C-D-E-F-G is five letter names, so the interval is a 5th.

topics.title: Perfect, Major, and Minor Intervals
topics.shortDescription: The most common interval qualities and how to identify them.
topics.longDescription: Within the major scale, intervals from the tonic are either perfect (1, 4, 5, 8) or major (2, 3, 6, 7). Minor intervals are major intervals lowered by a half step.
objectives: Identify perfect, major, and minor intervals on the staff
Build any major or minor interval above any note
Use the major scale as a reference for measuring intervals
commonMistakes: Forgetting which interval numbers can be perfect (1, 4, 5, 8) and which can be major/minor (2, 3, 6, 7)
practiceTips: If you can build a major scale on the bottom note, you can read off all the major and perfect intervals above it directly

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 31: Generic and Specific Intervals
Music Theory Lesson 32: Perfect and Major Intervals
Music Theory Lesson 33: Minor, Diminished, and Augmented Intervals

questions.questionText: C to E is a what interval?
questions.explanation: C to E is a third (3 letter names), and it contains 4 half steps, making it a major 3rd.

questions.questionText: Which intervals can be 'perfect'?
questions.explanation: Perfect quality applies only to unisons, 4ths, 5ths, and octaves.

topics.title: Augmented and Diminished Intervals
topics.shortDescription: Intervals one half step larger than perfect/major (augmented) or smaller than perfect/minor (diminished).
topics.longDescription: Augmenting and diminishing extends the interval system beyond perfect/major/minor. These rarer qualities are essential for chromatic harmony and accurate spelling.
objectives: Build augmented and diminished intervals above any note
Recognize them on the staff
Understand why enharmonic spelling matters (e.g., A♯ vs B♭ in interval context)
commonMistakes: Spelling enharmonically wrong: writing C-G♭ as a tritone when the context demands C-F♯
practiceTips: Always check that the number is correct first, then adjust the quality

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 33: Minor, Diminished, and Augmented Intervals

assessments.title: Augmented and Diminished Quiz
assessments.description: Test your knowledge of these qualities.

questions.questionText: C to F♯ is a what?
questions.explanation: C-D-E-F is four letter names (a 4th). A perfect 4th from C is F; F♯ raises it by a half step, making it an augmented 4th (also known as a tritone).

topics.title: Compound Intervals
topics.shortDescription: Intervals larger than an octave (9ths, 10ths, 11ths, etc.).
topics.longDescription: Compound intervals are simple intervals plus an octave. Understanding the relationship lets you analyze any large interval by reducing it to its simple form.
objectives: Identify compound intervals up to the 13th
Reduce compound intervals to simple intervals for analysis
commonMistakes: Miscounting when the interval spans more than an octave
practiceTips: Subtract 7 from the compound number to get the simple equivalent (a 10th = 3rd + octave)

questions.questionText: A 10th is equivalent to which simple interval plus an octave?
questions.explanation: 10 - 7 = 3. A 10th is a 3rd compounded by one octave.

topics.title: Interval Inversion
topics.shortDescription: When you flip an interval, its number and quality change predictably.
topics.longDescription: Inverting an interval (moving the bottom note up an octave, or the top note down) produces a complementary interval. Numbers sum to 9; major becomes minor, perfect stays perfect, augmented becomes diminished.
objectives: Predict the inversion of any interval (number and quality)
Use inversion to simplify identification of unfamiliar intervals
practiceTips: Remember the rule of nine: the original interval number plus its inversion always equals nine

questions.questionText: When inverted, a major 3rd becomes:
questions.explanation: Numbers sum to 9 (3 + 6 = 9), and major becomes minor on inversion.

topics.title: Identifying Intervals by Ear
topics.shortDescription: Train your ear to recognize intervals using reference songs and active listening.
topics.longDescription: Aural interval recognition is a core ear-training skill. The classic approach uses well-known song openings as memory hooks (e.g., 'Here Comes the Bride' = perfect 4th).
objectives: Reliably identify all simple intervals played melodically
Begin recognizing harmonic (simultaneous) intervals
commonMistakes: Relying only on song associations without internalizing the actual sound — eventually you should hear the interval directly
practiceTips: Practice daily, even 5–10 minutes, with a randomizing tool
Sing intervals back as well as identifying them

questions.questionText: Which is the most effective way to build long-term interval recognition?
questions.explanation: Short, frequent sessions with immediate feedback build aural recognition far more effectively than infrequent long sessions.

sections.title: Triads and Basic Chords
sections.shortDescription: Build and identify three-note chords in all qualities and inversions.
sections.longDescription: Triads are the workhorses of Western harmony. This section covers their construction, the four basic qualities (major, minor, diminished, augmented), their inversions, and figured bass notation.

topics.title: Building Triads
topics.shortDescription: A triad stacks three notes a third apart: root, third, and fifth.
topics.longDescription: Triads are three-note chords built in thirds. Every diatonic chord in basic harmony is a triad or extension of one. You'll learn the structure: root, third (major or minor), fifth (perfect, diminished, or augmented).
objectives: Define a triad as root, third, and fifth
Build a triad above any note
Identify the root from a triad in root position

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 40: Introduction to Chords

questions.questionText: A triad consists of:
questions.explanation: A triad is three notes stacked in thirds: root, third, and fifth.

topics.title: Major and Minor Triads
topics.shortDescription: The two most common chord qualities, distinguished by the third.
topics.longDescription: A major triad has a major third and perfect fifth above the root. A minor triad has a minor third and perfect fifth. These two qualities dominate Western tonal music.
objectives: Build major and minor triads from any root
Identify major and minor triads on the staff
Distinguish major and minor by ear
practiceTips: Sing the chord arpeggiated to feel the difference between major (bright) and minor (dark)

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 40: Introduction to Chords

questions.questionText: Which triad consists of a root, major third, and perfect fifth?
questions.explanation: Major triad = root + major 3rd + perfect 5th.

topics.title: Diminished and Augmented Triads
topics.shortDescription: Less common triad qualities created by altering the fifth.
topics.longDescription: Diminished triads have a minor third and diminished fifth (both 'lowered' compared to major). Augmented triads have a major third and augmented fifth. Both have a characteristic unstable sound.
objectives: Build diminished and augmented triads from any root
Identify all four triad qualities (major, minor, diminished, augmented) on the staff
Recognize the symmetry of the augmented triad (stacked major thirds)

questions.questionText: An augmented triad consists of which intervals from the root?
questions.explanation: Augmented triad = root + major 3rd + augmented 5th, or equivalently two stacked major thirds.

topics.title: Triad Inversions
topics.shortDescription: Rearrange chord tones so a note other than the root is on the bottom.
topics.longDescription: A chord is inverted when its third or fifth (rather than its root) sits in the bass. First inversion has the third in the bass; second inversion has the fifth in the bass. Inversions change the chord's sound and function without changing what chord it is.
objectives: Identify root position, first inversion, and second inversion of any triad
Recognize the appropriate figured bass symbols (5/3, 6/3, 6/4)
Understand how inversion affects voicing and bass line
commonMistakes: Confusing inversion with chord identity — a C major chord is still a C major chord in any inversion

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 42: Triad Inversion and Figured Bass

questions.questionText: First inversion of a triad means:
questions.explanation: First inversion places the third of the chord in the bass.

topics.title: Figured Bass Basics
topics.shortDescription: A shorthand from the Baroque era for indicating chords above a bass note.
topics.longDescription: Figured bass uses numbers (and sometimes accidentals) below a bass note to specify the chord above it. Modern theory uses it primarily as a shorthand for inversions.
objectives: Read basic figured bass symbols (5/3, 6, 6/4, 7, 6/5, 4/3, 4/2)
Realize a simple figured bass at a keyboard or on paper
commonMistakes: Treating figured bass as obsolete — it's still the standard way to label inversions in classical analysis

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 42: Triad Inversion and Figured Bass

questions.questionText: The figured bass symbol '6' (or 6/3) indicates which inversion?
questions.explanation: A '6' alone (short for 6/3) indicates first inversion: the bass is the third of the chord, with a 6th and a 3rd above it.

sections.title: Diatonic Harmony
sections.shortDescription: Build and analyze chord progressions using only the notes of one key.
sections.longDescription: This section moves from individual chords to chord progressions. You'll learn diatonic triads in major and minor, Roman numeral analysis, harmonic function (T-S-D), and cadences — the backbone of tonal harmony.

topics.title: Diatonic Triads in Major Keys
topics.shortDescription: The seven triads built on each scale degree of a major key.
topics.longDescription: Every scale degree supports a triad using only notes from the scale. In major: I (major), ii (minor), iii (minor), IV (major), V (major), vi (minor), vii° (diminished). This pattern is the foundation of diatonic harmony.
objectives: Build all seven diatonic triads in any major key
Identify the quality of each diatonic triad (I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°)
Memorize the pattern: M-m-m-M-M-m-d
practiceTips: Practice in multiple keys, not just C major

assessments.title: Diatonic Triads in Major Quiz
assessments.description: Test your knowledge of diatonic triads.

questions.questionText: In a major key, the chord built on the 5th scale degree (V) is what quality?
questions.explanation: In a major key, the V chord is major. This is what creates the strong dominant-to-tonic pull.

topics.title: Diatonic Triads in Minor Keys
topics.shortDescription: Triads built on each degree of a minor scale, with the harmonic minor's raised 7th.
topics.longDescription: Minor keys have more variation because of the three forms of the scale. In natural minor: i, ii°, III, iv, v, VI, VII. In harmonic minor (most common in classical practice): i, ii°, III+, iv, V, VI, vii°.
objectives: Build the diatonic triads in natural and harmonic minor
Understand why classical practice prefers harmonic minor's raised 7th for V
Identify chords in minor by Roman numeral
commonMistakes: Forgetting that the V chord in minor is typically major (using the raised 7th), not minor

questions.questionText: In a minor key using the harmonic form, the V chord is what quality?
questions.explanation: Raising the 7th scale degree in harmonic minor turns the v chord into a V (major), which provides the leading tone pull to the tonic.

topics.title: Roman Numeral Analysis
topics.shortDescription: Label chords by their function in a key using Roman numerals.
topics.longDescription: Roman numeral analysis labels each chord by the scale degree of its root, with case indicating quality (uppercase = major, lowercase = minor, with ° for diminished and + for augmented). It's the lingua franca of tonal analysis.
objectives: Label any diatonic chord with the correct Roman numeral and figure
Translate between chord names (e.g., 'F major') and Roman numerals in a key
Read a short progression and write its Roman numerals
commonMistakes: Forgetting that case matters: 'V' and 'v' are different chords
practiceTips: Always identify the key first before analyzing chords

resources.title: Music Theory Lesson 43: Roman Numeral Analysis

questions.questionText: In the key of G major, the chord D-F♯-A is labeled:
questions.explanation: D is the 5th scale degree of G major, and the chord is major (D-F♯-A), so it's V.

topics.title: Harmonic Function: Tonic, Subdominant, Dominant
topics.shortDescription: Group chords into three functional categories that drive tonal motion.
topics.longDescription: Tonic (T) chords feel stable and at rest (I, vi). Subdominant or predominant (S/PD) chords prepare motion (IV, ii). Dominant (D) chords create tension that wants to resolve to tonic (V, vii°). Understanding function is more useful than memorizing individual chord progressions.
objectives: Classify diatonic chords by function
Predict which chord can follow which
Explain why I-IV-V-I is the prototypical functional progression

questions.questionText: Which chord functions primarily as a dominant?
questions.explanation: V is the prototypical dominant chord, creating tension that resolves to the tonic.

topics.title: Cadences
topics.shortDescription: Harmonic punctuation: how phrases end.
topics.longDescription: Cadences are the harmonic equivalent of periods, commas, and question marks. You'll learn the four main types: authentic (V-I), plagal (IV-I), half (anything-V), and deceptive (V-vi).
objectives: Identify authentic, plagal, half, and deceptive cadences
Distinguish perfect from imperfect authentic cadences
Recognize cadences by ear and in score

questions.questionText: A V-I cadence with the tonic in the soprano and root position chords is called:
questions.explanation: A perfect authentic cadence (PAC) requires V-I with both chords in root position and the tonic in the soprano.

sections.title: Seventh Chords and Extensions
sections.shortDescription: Add sevenths and beyond to triads for richer functional and color harmonies.
sections.longDescription: Seventh chords expand the triadic vocabulary and are crucial for both classical voice leading and jazz harmony. This section covers all five seventh chord qualities, the dominant seventh, diatonic sevenths, and extensions through the 13th.

topics.title: Seventh Chord Types
topics.shortDescription: Add a seventh above a triad for the five common seventh chord qualities.
topics.longDescription: Adding a seventh above a triad creates a seventh chord. The five common types are major 7, dominant 7, minor 7, half-diminished 7, and fully diminished 7.
objectives: Build all five seventh chord qualities from any root
Identify them on the staff
Spell them with correct enharmonics
practiceTips: Always check both the triad quality and the seventh quality

questions.questionText: A dominant seventh chord consists of:
questions.explanation: Dominant 7th = major triad with a minor seventh above the root.

topics.title: The Dominant Seventh Chord
topics.shortDescription: The V7 chord and why it's the engine of tonal harmony.
topics.longDescription: The dominant seventh contains both the leading tone and the seventh, creating a powerful tritone that demands resolution to the tonic. It's arguably the single most important chord in functional harmony.
objectives: Build V7 in any key
Resolve V7 to I correctly (leading tone up, 7th down)
Recognize V7 in different inversions (V7, V6/5, V4/3, V4/2)
commonMistakes: Failing to resolve the leading tone upward in standard voice leading

questions.questionText: In the key of C major, V7 contains which notes?
questions.explanation: V7 in C major is built on G: G-B-D-F.

topics.title: Diatonic Seventh Chords
topics.shortDescription: Seventh chords built on each degree of major and minor scales.
topics.longDescription: Just as triads can be built on every scale degree, so can seventh chords. The qualities form a predictable pattern in major: IΔ7, ii7, iii7, IVΔ7, V7, vi7, vii⌀7.
objectives: Identify all diatonic seventh chords in major and minor keys
Use Roman numerals correctly for seventh chords

questions.questionText: In a major key, the seventh chord built on scale degree 1 is:
questions.explanation: The chord on scale degree 1 in major is a major triad plus a major 7th — a major 7th chord (IΔ7).

topics.title: Extended Chords: 9ths, 11ths, 13ths
topics.shortDescription: Stack thirds beyond the seventh for richer harmonies.
topics.longDescription: Extended chords add 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths above a seventh chord. They're essential in jazz and many popular styles, and they appear in late Romantic classical music as well.
objectives: Build 9th, 11th, and 13th chords
Understand which extensions are natural vs. altered
Recognize extension notation in lead sheets (e.g., Cmaj9, G13)
commonMistakes: Including all chord tones in dense voicings — extensions are typically used selectively

questions.questionText: A 13th chord theoretically contains how many distinct chord tones?
questions.explanation: A 13th chord stacks thirds: root, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th — 7 distinct tones (though many voicings omit some).

sections.title: Voice Leading and Part Writing
sections.shortDescription: Move individual voices smoothly between chords following classical principles.
sections.longDescription: This section focuses on how chords are connected through voice motion. You'll learn SATB part writing, the rules of voice leading (including the prohibition of parallels), and how non-chord tones add melodic interest above the harmony.

topics.title: SATB Voicing
topics.shortDescription: Distribute chord tones across four voices: soprano, alto, tenor, bass.
topics.longDescription: Common-practice harmony textbooks teach part writing in four-voice SATB format because it cleanly demonstrates voice leading principles. Each voice has a typical range and is notated on a specific staff position.
objectives: Notate SATB on grand staff with correct stem directions
Stay within the typical range of each voice
Double the correct chord tones (usually root or fifth)
commonMistakes: Doubling the leading tone (almost always wrong)
Crossing voices unintentionally

questions.questionText: In a four-part texture, which chord tone is typically NOT doubled?

options.optionText: The third (when it's the leading tone)

questions.explanation: Doubling the leading tone creates parallel octaves when both resolve up by step — it's a fundamental voice leading prohibition.

topics.title: Voice Leading Principles
topics.shortDescription: Smooth motion between chords avoids parallels and respects independence.
topics.longDescription: Voice leading is the art of moving individual voices smoothly between chords. The classical rules forbid parallel fifths and octaves, encourage contrary motion, and prefer the smallest motion in each voice.
objectives: Avoid parallel fifths and octaves between any two voices
Prefer stepwise motion when possible
Resolve leading tones up and chord sevenths down
commonMistakes: Allowing parallel fifths/octaves
Leaving the leading tone unresolved
practiceTips: Check each pair of voices separately for parallels

questions.questionText: Which is a primary voice leading prohibition in common-practice harmony?
questions.explanation: Parallel perfect fifths and octaves are the classic prohibitions because they undermine voice independence.

topics.title: Part Writing Common Practice Rules
topics.shortDescription: Apply voice leading to real chord progressions in four parts.
topics.longDescription: Part writing is the practical application of voice leading rules. You'll work through progressions like I-IV-V-I, applying rules for doubling, voice ranges, voice motion, and resolution.
objectives: Voice a progression in SATB correctly
Handle resolution of V7 to I properly
Manage common voice leading challenges (e.g., V to vi deceptive cadence)
practiceTips: Many part writing errors compound — fix the bass line first, then the upper voices

questions.questionText: In V7 to I in a major key, the leading tone (the 3rd of V7) should:

options.optionText: Resolve up by step to the tonic

questions.explanation: The leading tone resolves up by half step to the tonic — that's its defining function.

topics.title: Non-Chord Tones
topics.shortDescription: Notes that don't belong to the prevailing chord, used for melodic interest.
topics.longDescription: Non-chord tones (or non-harmonic tones) are pitches outside the current chord. They have specific names based on how they're approached and left: passing tones, neighbor tones, suspensions, anticipations, escape tones, appoggiaturas, and more.
objectives: Identify each type of non-chord tone in score
Use them appropriately in composition
Distinguish a true non-chord tone from a chord change

questions.questionText: A note approached by step and left by step in the same direction is a:
questions.explanation: Passing tones are approached and left by step in the same direction, 'passing' between two chord tones.

sections.title: Modes and Modal Harmony
sections.shortDescription: Beyond major and minor: the seven diatonic modes and how to harmonize them.
sections.longDescription: Modes are sister scales to major and minor, each with a distinctive character. This section introduces all seven diatonic modes and the principles of modal harmony as used in folk, jazz, film music, and many popular styles.

topics.title: Introduction to Modes
topics.shortDescription: Modes are scales derived by starting the major scale on different degrees.
topics.longDescription: The seven diatonic modes are essentially the major scale played starting from each of its seven scale degrees. Each mode has a unique interval pattern and character.
objectives: Understand the historical and theoretical origin of modes
Recognize that modes differ from each other in interval patterns
Distinguish modal music from tonal major/minor
commonMistakes: Treating modes as just 'starting the scale on a different note' without understanding tonal centers

questions.questionText: How many diatonic modes are there?
questions.explanation: There are seven diatonic modes, one for each scale degree of the major scale: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, and Locrian.

topics.title: The Seven Diatonic Modes
topics.shortDescription: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian.
topics.longDescription: Each mode has a characteristic sound. Ionian = major. Dorian = minor with raised 6. Phrygian = minor with lowered 2. Lydian = major with raised 4. Mixolydian = major with lowered 7. Aeolian = natural minor. Locrian = minor with lowered 2 and lowered 5.
objectives: Build each mode from any starting note
Identify the characteristic ('avoid' or 'color') note of each mode
Recognize each mode by ear
practiceTips: Compare each mode to its parallel major or minor to spot the characteristic alteration
Listen to clear modal examples (e.g., 'Scarborough Fair' = Dorian, 'Norwegian Wood' verse = Mixolydian)

questions.questionText: Dorian mode is most similar to which scale?
questions.explanation: Dorian is a minor mode (minor 3rd) but has a raised 6th compared to natural minor.

questions.questionText: Mixolydian mode is most similar to which scale?
questions.explanation: Mixolydian is a major mode (major 3rd) but has a lowered 7th compared to the major scale — giving it that bluesy, folky sound.

topics.title: Modal Harmony
topics.shortDescription: Build chord progressions that establish and reinforce a modal center.
topics.longDescription: Modal harmony differs from tonal harmony because it doesn't use a strong V-I cadence. Instead, it emphasizes the characteristic tones of the mode through chord choices and bass motion.
objectives: Identify common chord progressions for each mode
Compose simple modal progressions
Recognize modal harmony in popular and folk music
commonMistakes: Using V-I cadences in modal contexts — that turns the music tonal, not modal

questions.questionText: Why is V-i avoided in modal harmony?

options.optionText: It establishes tonal rather than modal feel
It can't be played in minor

questions.explanation: The strong V-i (with leading tone) is the hallmark of tonal harmony. Modal music typically uses bVII or other progressions that avoid that pull.

sections.title: Chromatic Harmony
sections.shortDescription: Expand beyond diatonic harmony with secondary dominants, borrowed chords, and modulation.
sections.longDescription: Chromatic harmony enriches tonal music with chords outside the immediate key. This section covers secondary dominants, modal mixture, Neapolitan and augmented sixth chords, and modulation to new keys.

topics.title: Secondary Dominants
topics.shortDescription: Borrow a V chord that points to a chord other than the tonic.
topics.longDescription: Secondary dominants temporarily treat a non-tonic chord as a tonic by preceding it with its own V (or V7). 'V of V', 'V of vi', etc. expand the harmonic palette without leaving the original key.
objectives: Build any secondary dominant in any key
Identify secondary dominants in score and notate them correctly (e.g., V/V)
Resolve them properly
commonMistakes: Forgetting that secondary dominants typically resolve to a major or minor triad (not a diminished triad), so V/vii° is rare

questions.questionText: In the key of C major, what chord would V/V be?
questions.explanation: V of V means the dominant of G (since G is the V of C). The V of G is D major.

topics.title: Borrowed Chords (Modal Mixture)
topics.shortDescription: Borrow chords from the parallel minor (or major) for color.
topics.longDescription: Modal mixture or 'borrowing' uses chords from the parallel mode. In a major key, borrowing from parallel minor gives you bIII, bVI, bVII, iv, and more. These add chromatic color without modulating.
objectives: Identify common borrowed chords (bVI, iv in major, etc.)
Notate them correctly in Roman numerals
Recognize the characteristic 'darker' color they add

questions.questionText: In C major, the chord A♭-C-E♭ is best analyzed as:
questions.explanation: A♭ major in the key of C major is a borrowed chord — it's the VI of C minor (the parallel minor), borrowed and used in major. We label it bVI.

topics.title: Neapolitan and Augmented Sixth Chords
topics.shortDescription: Two specialized chromatic chords with strong dominant function.
topics.longDescription: The Neapolitan (bII, usually in first inversion) and the augmented sixth chords (Italian, French, German) are powerful pre-dominant chords used in classical practice to enrich approaches to V.
objectives: Build a Neapolitan chord in major and minor keys
Build Italian, French, and German augmented sixth chords
Recognize and analyze these chords in score

questions.questionText: The Neapolitan chord is built on which scale degree?
questions.explanation: The Neapolitan is a major triad built on the lowered (flatted) 2nd scale degree. It typically appears in first inversion (N6).

topics.title: Modulation
topics.shortDescription: Move from one key to another mid-piece.
topics.longDescription: Modulation is changing keys during a piece. You'll learn the most common types: common chord (pivot chord) modulation, direct modulation, sequential modulation, and modulation through chromaticism.
objectives: Identify modulations in a score
Use common chord modulation in composition
Distinguish modulation from temporary tonicization (which doesn't establish a new key)
commonMistakes: Calling a passing secondary dominant a 'modulation' — true modulation establishes a new key with at least a phrase of new material

questions.questionText: A common chord modulation uses:

options.optionText: A chord that exists in both the old and new keys

questions.explanation: Common chord modulation pivots on a chord that is diatonic to both keys, allowing a smooth shift.

sections.title: Form and Analysis
sections.shortDescription: How pieces are structured at every scale, from phrases to symphonies.
sections.longDescription: Form is the architecture of music. This section covers phrase and period structure, small forms (binary, ternary), large classical forms (sonata, rondo, theme and variations), and popular music forms (verse-chorus, AABA, 12-bar blues).

topics.title: Phrase and Period Structure
topics.shortDescription: The smallest units of musical form: phrases combine into periods.
topics.longDescription: A phrase is a complete musical thought ending in a cadence. Two phrases that 'pair up' (often question/answer) form a period. Understanding phrase structure is the basis for analyzing larger forms.
objectives: Identify phrases and their cadences
Distinguish a parallel period from a contrasting period
Recognize antecedent-consequent relationships

questions.questionText: A period typically consists of:

options.optionText: Two phrases ending in different cadences

questions.explanation: A standard period is two phrases: an antecedent (often ending on a half cadence) and a consequent (typically ending on an authentic cadence).

topics.title: Binary and Ternary Forms
topics.shortDescription: Two-part (AB) and three-part (ABA) structures.
topics.longDescription: Binary form has two main sections (A and B), often each repeated. Ternary form has three sections where the third returns to the first (ABA). Both are foundational small forms in Western music.
objectives: Identify binary and ternary structures in scores
Distinguish simple from rounded binary
Recognize both forms by ear

questions.questionText: Ternary form is best described as:
questions.explanation: Ternary form is ABA: a section, a contrasting section, and a return of the first section.

topics.title: Sonata Form
topics.shortDescription: The most important large form of the Classical era.
topics.longDescription: Sonata form has three main parts: exposition (two contrasting themes in different keys), development (working out of material), and recapitulation (return of themes in the home key). It's used in the first movement of countless symphonies, sonatas, and chamber works.
objectives: Identify exposition, development, and recapitulation in a sonata movement
Recognize the first and second theme groups
Track tonal motion (modulation in exposition, return in recapitulation)
practiceTips: Listen with a score in hand — sonata form is much easier to see than to hear at first

questions.questionText: In a major-key sonata-form exposition, the second theme typically appears in which key?
questions.explanation: In a major-key sonata exposition, the second theme is normally in the dominant key. In a minor-key sonata, it's typically in the relative major.

topics.title: Rondo and Theme and Variations
topics.shortDescription: Two other important classical forms.
topics.longDescription: Rondo alternates a recurring theme (A) with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.): ABACA or ABACABA. Theme and variations presents a theme and then transforms it through successive variations.
objectives: Identify rondo and theme-and-variations forms
Recognize how a theme is varied (melodic, harmonic, rhythmic, textural, etc.)

questions.questionText: The defining feature of rondo form is:

options.optionText: A theme that recurs between contrasting episodes

questions.explanation: Rondo is characterized by a refrain (A) returning between contrasting episodes.

topics.title: Popular Music Forms
topics.shortDescription: Verse-chorus, AABA, 12-bar blues, and other popular structures.
topics.longDescription: Popular music has its own form vocabulary: verse-chorus (with optional bridge, pre-chorus, etc.), AABA (the 'standard' Tin Pan Alley form), 12-bar blues, and various others. Knowing these is essential for songwriting and listening analytically.
objectives: Identify verse-chorus and AABA structures by ear and on paper
Recognize the 12-bar blues progression
Understand how bridges and pre-choruses function

questions.questionText: AABA refers to a song form where:

options.optionText: Two A sections, a bridge (B), and a final A

questions.explanation: AABA features two A sections (verses), a contrasting B section (bridge), and a final A. Common in jazz standards and many older pop songs.

sections.title: Counterpoint
sections.shortDescription: Combine multiple independent melodic lines using species counterpoint.
sections.longDescription: Counterpoint trains your ear and pen for the interaction of melodies. This section walks through the five species of counterpoint, building from note-against-note to florid combination, and introduces imitation, canon, and the fundamentals of fugue.

topics.title: Introduction to Counterpoint
topics.shortDescription: The art of combining independent melodic lines.
topics.longDescription: Counterpoint is the discipline of combining two or more independent melodies into a coherent whole. It dominates Renaissance and Baroque music and remains foundational for any composer or arranger.
objectives: Define counterpoint and distinguish it from homophony
Recognize counterpoint in real music (e.g., Bach inventions)
Understand why each voice should remain independent yet consonant with the others

questions.questionText: Counterpoint is primarily concerned with:

options.optionText: The relationship between simultaneous independent melodic lines

questions.explanation: Counterpoint focuses on how independent melodic lines interact when sounding together.

topics.title: First Species Counterpoint
topics.shortDescription: Note against note: one note in the counterpoint for each note in the cantus firmus.
topics.longDescription: First species counterpoint, codified by Fux, teaches the basics of consonance, contrary motion, and avoiding parallels by reducing the problem to its simplest form: note-against-note motion.
objectives: Compose a first-species counterpoint above and below a cantus firmus
Use only consonant intervals (with care around 8ves and 5ths)
Favor contrary and oblique motion; avoid parallel perfect intervals
practiceTips: Sing both lines together at the keyboard if possible — checking by ear catches problems theory alone might miss

questions.questionText: In first species counterpoint, which intervals are typically used between the two voices?
questions.explanation: First species is strictly consonant note-against-note: perfect 5ths, octaves, and 3rds/6ths (with care).

topics.title: Second through Fifth Species
topics.shortDescription: Adding more notes per measure and introducing dissonance.
topics.longDescription: Species counterpoint progresses from note-against-note to more elaborate combinations: two notes against one (second), four against one (third), syncopation and suspensions (fourth), and free combination (fifth, also called florid).
objectives: Compose second, third, fourth, and fifth species counterpoint
Use passing tones and suspensions correctly
Combine multiple species in florid counterpoint

questions.questionText: Fourth species counterpoint is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Fourth species introduces syncopation and the use of suspensions (dissonance prepared by consonance, then resolved by step).

topics.title: Imitation and Canon
topics.shortDescription: When one voice imitates another, sometimes strictly (canon).
topics.longDescription: Imitation is when one voice restates material from another, possibly transposed or transformed. A canon is strict imitation throughout. These techniques underpin much of Renaissance and Baroque music, including fugues.
objectives: Identify imitation in a score
Write a simple two-voice canon at the unison or octave
Understand the basics of fugue (subject, answer, countersubject)

questions.questionText: A canon is:
questions.explanation: A canon features strict imitation where one voice continues to imitate another throughout the piece.

sections.title: Ear Training
sections.shortDescription: Train your aural skills in parallel with everything else.
sections.longDescription: Ear training is a parallel track that develops alongside theoretical knowledge. This section covers interval recognition, chord quality, scale and mode identification, and melodic and harmonic dictation. Practice daily, even briefly, throughout your theory studies.

topics.title: Interval Ear Training
topics.shortDescription: Recognize ascending, descending, and harmonic intervals by ear.
topics.longDescription: Ear training for intervals starts with the basics and works up to fluent recognition of all qualities both melodically (one note at a time) and harmonically (simultaneously).
objectives: Reliably identify all ascending intervals up to an octave
Identify descending intervals
Begin identifying harmonic intervals
practiceTips: Daily short sessions beat occasional long ones
Sing back every interval you hear

topics.title: Chord Quality Recognition
topics.shortDescription: Identify chord types by ear: major, minor, diminished, augmented, dominant 7th, etc.
topics.longDescription: Chord quality recognition is the next layer of ear training. Start with major vs. minor triads, then add diminished and augmented, then seventh chords.
objectives: Distinguish major, minor, diminished, and augmented triads by ear
Identify the five seventh chord qualities by ear
Recognize chord inversions (more advanced)

topics.title: Scale and Mode Recognition
topics.shortDescription: Identify scales and modes by ear.
topics.longDescription: Scale recognition usually starts with major vs. minor, then extends to minor variants and modes. The characteristic tones of each scale are the key recognition handles.
objectives: Distinguish major and minor scales by ear
Identify the three forms of minor
Begin identifying modes by ear

topics.title: Melodic Dictation
topics.shortDescription: Hear a melody and write it down.
topics.longDescription: Melodic dictation combines pitch recognition, rhythm reading, and notation skills. Start with short diatonic melodies in known keys and gradually add chromaticism and longer phrases.
objectives: Notate short diatonic melodies after hearing them a few times
Capture rhythm accurately
Identify the key and meter from listening
commonMistakes: Trying to write everything on first hearing — listen for the overall shape first
practiceTips: Establish the key and time signature before you start writing notes
Sketch the rhythm first with neutral pitches, then go back and fill in the pitches

topics.title: Harmonic Dictation
topics.shortDescription: Hear a chord progression and write it down with Roman numerals.
topics.longDescription: Harmonic dictation builds on chord-quality recognition by adding the dimension of function. Listen for the bass line first, then identify the chord above it.
objectives: Notate the bass line of a heard progression
Identify the chord on each beat
Write the progression with Roman numerals
practiceTips: Bass line first, soprano second, inner voices last
Look for functional patterns (T-S-D-T) rather than guessing chord by chord

sections.title: Advanced and Contemporary Topics
sections.shortDescription: Jazz harmony, altered chords, atonality, and pitch-class set theory.
sections.longDescription: Beyond common-practice tonality lies a rich world of harmonic systems. This section introduces jazz harmony, altered chords and reharmonization, atonal music, and the basics of pitch-class set theory.

topics.title: Jazz Harmony Fundamentals
topics.shortDescription: Chord-scale relationships, ii-V-I progressions, and lead sheet notation.
topics.longDescription: Jazz harmony extends classical theory with frequent seventh and extended chords, the ii-V-I as a basic unit, and chord-scale theory for improvisation. You'll learn the basics needed to read a lead sheet and understand jazz progressions.
objectives: Read lead sheet chord symbols fluently (Cmaj7, G7♭9, Dm11, etc.)
Recognize ii-V-I and its variants in major and minor
Understand the basic chord-scale relationship

questions.questionText: ii-V-I in C major is which chords?
questions.explanation: ii in C is Dm7, V is G7, and I is Cmaj7.

topics.title: Altered Chords and Reharmonization
topics.shortDescription: Alter chord tones or substitute chords for harmonic interest.
topics.longDescription: Altered chords (typically dominant 7ths with raised or lowered 5ths and 9ths) provide chromatic color and tension. Reharmonization replaces chords in a progression with substitutes that retain or transform the original function.
objectives: Identify and use altered dominants (♭9, ♯9, ♯11, ♭13)
Apply tritone substitution
Reharmonize a simple progression in multiple ways

questions.questionText: Tritone substitution replaces a dominant 7th chord with another dominant 7th whose root is:
questions.explanation: Tritone sub swaps a V7 with the V7 a tritone away, since they share the same tritone (3rd and 7th) — e.g., G7 ↔ Db7.

topics.title: Introduction to Atonality
topics.shortDescription: Music without a tonal center: the breakdown of common-practice harmony.
topics.longDescription: In the early 20th century, composers like Schoenberg, Webern, and Berg abandoned functional harmony. Atonal music treats all 12 pitches as equal, leading to new analytical approaches.
objectives: Define atonality and contrast it with tonality
Recognize atonal music aurally
Identify the historical and aesthetic motivations behind atonality

questions.questionText: Atonal music is characterized by:

options.optionText: The absence of a tonal center
Use of only the major scale

questions.explanation: Atonal music deliberately avoids establishing any single pitch as a tonal center.

topics.title: Set Theory Basics
topics.shortDescription: An analytical method for atonal music using pitch-class sets.
topics.longDescription: Pitch-class set theory analyzes atonal music by grouping pitches into sets (collections) and studying their structural relationships using integer notation (0–11) and operations like transposition and inversion.
objectives: Convert pitches to pitch-class integers
Find the normal form and prime form of a set
Apply basic transposition and inversion operations
commonMistakes: Confusing 'set' in this context with 'scale' — sets need not be ordered

questions.questionText: In pitch-class set theory, the number 0 represents which pitch class?
questions.explanation: By convention, pitch class 0 = C. The integers 0 through 11 represent C, C#, D, D#, E, F, F#, G, G#, A, A#, B.
